meta.title: The Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tree covering locus, orientation, and radius in sequence.

labels.external: more shaped by circumstance than choice

labels.entitlement: focused more on what was owed to you
labels.contribution: focused more on what you added

nodes.text: Good evening. Let's take a clear look at your day.
Which word fits today best?

options.label: Productive
Mixed
Tough
Draining

ifAnswerIn: Productive
Mixed
Tough
Draining

nodes.text: You called the day "{OPEN_TONE.answer}". When something went well, what made it happen?

options.label: I prepared well
I adapted when plans changed
The team carried it
Honestly, I got lucky

nodes.text: You called the day "{OPEN_TONE.answer}". When things got difficult, what was your first instinct?

options.label: Figure out what I could control
Push through with what I had
Wait for someone to step in
Feel stuck and replay the problem

ifAnswerIn: I prepared well
I adapted when plans changed
The team carried it
Honestly, I got lucky
Figure out what I could control
Push through with what I had
Wait for someone to step in
Feel stuck and replay the problem

nodes.text: Where did you notice your choice most clearly today?

options.label: In how I prepared
In how I responded under pressure
In asking for help instead of freezing
I still mostly reacted to what others did

nodes.text: Looking back, where was your smallest point of choice today?

options.label: I could have clarified expectations earlier
I could have changed my response, even if not the situation
There really wasn't much I could do
I mostly needed someone else to fix it

nodes.text: You saw some of your hand in the day. That does not mean the day was easy. It means you noticed a lever: preparation, response, or asking for help. Agency often starts that small.

A hard day can make everything feel imposed from the outside. That is human. Your answers still hint at a hinge point: not full control, but some choice in how you interpreted, escalated, or responded.
Let's shift from how you handled the day to what you gave inside it.
Let's shift from what pressed on you today to what you still offered inside it.
Think about one interaction today. Which description fits best?

options.label: I helped someone even though I did not have to
I taught, shared, or unblocked someone
I felt overlooked for what I had already done
I was frustrated that others were not doing their part

ifAnswerIn: I helped someone even though I did not have to
I taught, shared, or unblocked someone
I felt overlooked for what I had already done
I was frustrated that others were not doing their part

nodes.text: What best captures the spirit of that moment?

options.label: I saw a need and stepped in
I wanted the work to move, not just my task
Part of me still wanted credit for it

nodes.text: When you felt overlooked or let down, what sat underneath that feeling?

options.label: I wanted recognition more than progress
I wanted fairness, but I still could have helped move things
I mostly stayed focused on what others owed
I turned that frustration into support for someone else

nodes.text: Your answers tilt toward contribution. You were not only tracking what you got from the day; you noticed where you added momentum, clarity, or care.

Your answers tilt toward entitlement. That does not make you selfish; it suggests your attention stayed close to recognition, fairness, or what others should have done. Useful to notice. Contribution often reopens movement.
One more shift: from what you gave to who you were holding in mind while you gave it.
One more shift: from what felt owed or effortful to who else was inside the frame of your day.
When you think about today's biggest challenge, who comes to mind first?

options.label: Mostly me and my workload
My team, because we were all affected
A specific colleague who had it harder
The customer or end user

ifAnswerIn: Mostly me and my workload
My team, because we were all affected
A specific colleague who had it harder
The customer or end user

nodes.text: What did that wider view change for you?

options.label: It made my frustration feel more workable
It helped me choose a more useful response
I noticed others, but I still stayed mostly in my own stress

nodes.text: If you widened the frame by one step, who would enter the picture next?

options.label: A teammate depending on my clarity
A customer affected by the result
Honestly, I still mostly see my own pressure

nodes.text: Tomorrow, what would be the healthiest next step?

options.label: Protect my own bandwidth first
Communicate so the team can move cleanly
Check on the person most affected
Reconnect my work to the user outcome

nodes.text: Your radius widened beyond yourself. That usually does not erase stress. It changes its size. When other people, shared work, or users stay in view, meaning gets easier to recover.

Your attention stayed close to your own load. That makes sense on a strained day. The invitation is not self-erasure; it is one step wider. Often, perspective reduces suffering before it solves the task.

Today you were {axis1.dominantLabel}, {axis2.dominantLabel}, and carried a {axis3.dominantLabel}. You described the day as "{OPEN_TONE.answer}", and your chosen next step is "{A3_Q_TOMORROW.answer}". A useful experiment for tomorrow: make one small move that shifts from reaction to intention, from expectation to contribution, and from self-focus to shared context.
Session complete. See you tomorrow.
